From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Series of alternating glacial and interglacial periods

This article is about the series of glacial periods during the last 2.58 million years. For the glacial period lasting from 110,000 to 11,700 years ago, see [Last Glacial Period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Iceage_north-glacial_hg.png/330px-Iceage_north-glacial_hg.png)](/wiki/File:Iceage_north-glacial_hg.png)

Extent of maximum glaciation (in black) in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") during the Pleistocene. The formation of 3 to 4 km (1.9 to 2.5 mi) thick ice sheets equate to a global sea level drop of about 120 m (390 ft)

The **Quaternary glaciation**, also known as the **Pleistocene glaciation**, is an alternating series of [glacial](/wiki/Glacial_period "Glacial period") and [interglacial periods](/wiki/Interglacial "Interglacial") during the [Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary") period that began 2.58 [Ma](/wiki/Year#SI_prefix_multipliers "Year") (million years ago) and is ongoing.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Quaternary_Science-3) Although geologists describe this entire period up to the present as an "[ice age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age")", in [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture") this term usually refers to the [most recent glacial period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period"), or to the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") epoch in general.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Since [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") still has polar [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet"), geologists consider the Quaternary glaciation to be ongoing, though currently in an interglacial period.

During the Quaternary glaciation, ice sheets appeared, expanding during glacial periods and contracting during interglacial periods. Since the end of the last glacial period, only the [Antarctic](/wiki/Antarctic_ice_sheet "Antarctic ice sheet") and [Greenland ice sheets](/wiki/Greenland_ice_sheet "Greenland ice sheet") have survived, while other sheets formed during glacial periods, such as the [Laurentide Ice Sheet](/wiki/Laurentide_Ice_Sheet "Laurentide Ice Sheet"), have completely melted.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Fig3_Q-climate-CO2-feedbacks_V2.png/250px-Fig3_Q-climate-CO2-feedbacks_V2.png)](/wiki/File:Fig3_Q-climate-CO2-feedbacks_V2.png)

Diagram of key climate-carbon cycle feedbacks linking Quaternary climate temperatures, Generalized Milankovitch Theory (GMT), to atmospheric CO2 and ice sheets.[[3]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Quaternary_Science-3) Positive feedbacks amplify and negative feedbacks dampen environmental change, with slow-acting responses shown as dashed arrows.

The major effects of the Quaternary glaciation have been the continental [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion") of land and the [deposition](/wiki/Deposition_(geology) "Deposition (geology)") of material; the modification of [river systems](/wiki/Drainage_system_(geomorphology) "Drainage system (geomorphology)"); the formation of millions of [lakes](/wiki/Lake "Lake"), including the development of [pluvial lakes](/wiki/Pluvial_lake "Pluvial lake") far from the ice margins; changes in [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"); the [isostatic adjustment](/wiki/Isostasy "Isostasy") of the Earth's [crust](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)"); flooding; and abnormal winds. The ice sheets, by raising the [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo") (the ratio of solar [radiant energy](/wiki/Radiant_energy "Radiant energy") reflected from Earth back into space), generated significant [feedback](/wiki/Feedback "Feedback") to further cool the [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate"). These effects have shaped land and ocean environments and biological communities.

Long before the Quaternary glaciation, land-based ice appeared and then disappeared during at least four other ice ages. The Quaternary glaciation can be considered a part of a [Late Cenozoic Ice Age](/wiki/Late_Cenozoic_Ice_Age "Late Cenozoic Ice Age") that began 33.9 Ma and is ongoing.

## Discovery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Discovery")]

Main article: [Ice age § Origin of ice age theory](/wiki/Ice_age#Origin_of_ice_age_theory "Ice age")

Evidence for the [Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary") glaciation was first understood in the 18th and 19th centuries as part of the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution"). Over the last century, extensive field observations have provided evidence that continental glaciers covered large parts of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), and [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"). Maps of glacial features were compiled after many years of fieldwork by hundreds of geologists who mapped the location and orientation of [drumlins](/wiki/Drumlin "Drumlin"), [eskers](/wiki/Esker "Esker"), [moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine"), [striations](/wiki/Striation_(geology) "Striation (geology)"), and [glacial stream](/wiki/Glacial_stream "Glacial stream") channels to reveal the extent of the [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet"), the direction of their flow, and the systems of [meltwater](/wiki/Meltwater "Meltwater") channels. They also allowed scientists to decipher a history of multiple advances and retreats of the ice. Even before the theory of worldwide glaciation was generally accepted, many observers recognized that more than a single advance and retreat of the ice had occurred.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

Further information: [Marine isotope stages](/wiki/Marine_isotope_stages "Marine isotope stages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Vostok_Petit_data.svg/500px-Vostok_Petit_data.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Vostok_Petit_data.svg)

Graph of reconstructed temperature (blue), CO2 (green), and dust (red) from the [Vostok Station](/wiki/Vostok_Station "Vostok Station") ice core for the past 420,000 years

To geologists, an [ice age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age") is defined by the presence of large amounts of land-based ice. Prior to the Quaternary glaciation, land-based ice formed during at least four earlier geologic periods: the [late Paleozoic](/wiki/Late_Paleozoic_icehouse "Late Paleozoic icehouse") (360–260 Ma), [Andean-Saharan](/wiki/Andean-Saharan_glaciation "Andean-Saharan glaciation") (450–420 Ma), [Cryogenian](/wiki/Cryogenian "Cryogenian") (720–635 Ma) and [Huronian](/wiki/Huronian_glaciation "Huronian glaciation") (2,400–2,100 Ma).[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Within the Quaternary ice age, there were also periodic fluctuations of the total volume of land ice, the sea level, and global temperatures. During the colder episodes (referred to as [glacial periods](/wiki/Glacial_period "Glacial period") or glacials) large ice sheets at least 4 km (2.5 mi) thick at their maximum covered parts of Europe, North America, and Siberia. The shorter warm intervals between glacials, when continental glaciers retreated, are referred to as [interglacials](/wiki/Interglacial "Interglacial"). These are evidenced by buried soil profiles, peat beds, and lake and stream deposits separating the unsorted, unstratified deposits of glacial debris.

Initially the glacial/interglacial cycle length was about 41,000 years, but following the [Mid-Pleistocene Transition](/wiki/Mid-Pleistocene_Transition "Mid-Pleistocene Transition") about 1 Ma, it slowed to about 100,000 years, as evidenced most clearly by [ice cores](/wiki/Ice_core "Ice core") for the past 800,000 years and marine sediment cores for the earlier period. Over the past 740,000 years there have been eight glacial cycles.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The entire Quaternary period, starting 2.58 Ma, is referred to as an ice age because at least one permanent large ice sheet—the [Antarctic ice sheet](/wiki/Antarctic_ice_sheet "Antarctic ice sheet")—has existed continuously. There is uncertainty over how much of [Greenland was covered by ice](/wiki/Greenland_ice_sheet "Greenland ice sheet") during each interglacial. Currently, Earth is in an interglacial period, the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") epoch beginning 11,700 years ago; this has caused the ice sheets from the [Last Glacial Period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") to [slowly melt](/wiki/Holocene_glacial_retreat "Holocene glacial retreat"). The remaining glaciers, now occupying about 10% of the world's land surface, cover Greenland, Antarctica and some mountainous regions. During the glacial periods, the present (i.e., interglacial) hydrologic system was completely interrupted throughout large areas of the world and was considerably modified in others. The volume of ice on land resulted in a sea level about 120 metres (394 ft) lower than present.

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Causes")]

Further information: [Ice age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age")

Earth's history of glaciation is a product of the *internal variability* of Earth's [climate system](/wiki/Climate_system "Climate system") (e.g., [ocean currents](/wiki/Ocean_currents "Ocean currents"), [carbon cycle](/wiki/Carbon_cycle "Carbon cycle")), interacting with *external forcing* by phenomena outside the climate system (e.g., [changes in Earth's orbit](/wiki/Orbital_forcing "Orbital forcing"), [volcanism](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism"), and changes in [solar output](/wiki/Solar_output "Solar output")).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Astronomical cycles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Astronomical cycles")]

Main articles: [Milankovitch cycles](/wiki/Milankovitch_cycles "Milankovitch cycles") and [orbital forcing](/wiki/Orbital_forcing "Orbital forcing")

See also: [100,000-year problem](/wiki/100,000-year_problem "100,000-year problem")

The role of Earth's orbital changes in controlling climate was first advanced by [James Croll](/wiki/James_Croll "James Croll") in the late 19th century.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Later, the Serbian [geophysicist](/wiki/Geophysics "Geophysics") [Milutin Milanković](/wiki/Milutin_Milankovi%C4%87 "Milutin Milanković") elaborated on the theory and calculated that these irregularities in Earth's orbit could cause the climatic cycles now known as [Milankovitch cycles](/wiki/Milankovitch_cycles "Milankovitch cycles").[[10]](#cite_note-10) They are the result of the additive behavior of several types of cyclical changes in Earth's orbital properties.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/7e/Milankovitch_Variations.png)](/wiki/File:Milankovitch_Variations.png)

Relationship of Earth's orbit to periods of glaciation

Firstly, changes in the [orbital eccentricity](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity") of Earth occur on a cycle of about 100,000 years.[[11]](#cite_note-ilwhy-11) Secondly, the [inclination](/wiki/Orbital_inclination "Orbital inclination") or tilt of Earth's axis varies between 22° and 24.5° in a cycle 41,000 years long.[[11]](#cite_note-ilwhy-11) The tilt of Earth's axis is responsible for the [seasons](/wiki/Season "Season"); the greater the tilt, the greater the contrast between summer and winter temperatures. Thirdly, [precession](/wiki/Precession "Precession") of the [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox"), or wobbles in the [Earth's rotation axis](/wiki/Axial_tilt "Axial tilt"), have a periodicity of 26,000 years. According to the Milankovitch theory, these factors cause a periodic cooling of Earth, with the coldest part in the cycle occurring about every 40,000 years. The main effect of the Milankovitch cycles is to change the contrast between the seasons, not the annual amount of solar heat Earth receives. The result is less ice melting than accumulating, and [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") build up.

Milankovitch worked out the ideas of climatic cycles in the 1920s and 1930s, but it was not until the 1970s that a sufficiently long and detailed chronology of the Quaternary temperature changes was worked out to test the theory adequately.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Studies of deep-sea cores and their fossils indicate that the fluctuation of climate during the last few hundred thousand years is remarkably close to that predicted by Milankovitch.

### Atmospheric composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Atmospheric composition")]

One theory holds that decreases in atmospheric [CO  
2](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide"), an important [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas"), started the long-term cooling trend that eventually led to the formation of continental ice sheets in the Arctic.[[13]](#cite_note-BartoliHönischZeebe2011-13) [Geological evidence](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide_in_Earth%27s_atmosphere#Concentrations_in_the_geologic_past "Carbon dioxide in Earth's atmosphere") indicates a decrease of more than 90% in atmospheric CO2 since the middle of the [Mesozoic Era](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic").[[14]](#cite_note-14) An analysis of CO2 reconstructions from [alkenone](/wiki/Alkenone "Alkenone") records shows that CO2 in the atmosphere declined before and during Antarctic glaciation, and supports a substantial CO2 decrease as the primary cause of Antarctic glaciation.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Decreasing carbon dioxide levels during the late Pliocene may have contributed substantially to global cooling and the onset of Northern Hemisphere glaciation.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Lunt082-17) This decrease in atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations may have come about by way of the decreasing ventilation of deep water in the Southern Ocean.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

CO2 levels also play an important role in the transitions between interglacials and glacials.[[19]](#cite_note-19) High CO2 contents correspond to warm interglacial periods, and low CO2 to glacial periods. However, studies indicate that CO  
2 may not be the primary cause of the interglacial-glacial transitions, but instead acts as a [feedback](/wiki/Feedback "Feedback").[[3]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Quaternary_Science-3) The explanation for this observed CO  
2 variation "remains a difficult attribution problem".[[20]](#cite_note-Joos-20)

### Plate tectonics and ocean currents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Plate tectonics and ocean currents")]

Further information: [Plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics") and [ocean current](/wiki/Ocean_current "Ocean current")

Video illustrating the beginning of glaciation (German)

An important component in the development of long-term ice ages is the positions of the continents.[[21]](#cite_note-21) These can control the circulation of the oceans and the atmosphere, affecting how ocean currents carry heat to high latitudes. Throughout most of [geologic time](/wiki/Geologic_time_scale "Geologic time scale"), the [North Pole](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") appears to have been in a broad, open ocean that allowed major ocean currents to move unabated. Equatorial waters flowed into the polar regions, warming them. This produced mild, uniform climates that persisted throughout most of geologic time.

But during the [Cenozoic Era](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic"), the large [North American](/wiki/North_American_Plate "North American Plate") and [South American](/wiki/South_American_Plate "South American Plate") continental plates drifted westward from the [Eurasian Plate](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate"). This interlocked with the development of the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), running north–south, with the North Pole in the small, nearly landlocked basin of the [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean"). The [Drake Passage](/wiki/Drake_Passage "Drake Passage") opened 33.9 million years ago (the [Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene")-[Oligocene](/wiki/Oligocene "Oligocene") transition), severing [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctic_Plate "Antarctic Plate") from South America. The [Antarctic Circumpolar Current](/wiki/Antarctic_Circumpolar_Current "Antarctic Circumpolar Current") could then flow through it, isolating Antarctica from warm waters and triggering the formation of its huge ice sheets. The weakening of the [North Atlantic Current](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Current "North Atlantic Current") (NAC) around 3.65 to 3.5 million years ago resulted in cooling and freshening of the Arctic Ocean, nurturing the development of Arctic sea ice and preconditioning the formation of continental glaciers later in the Pliocene.[[22]](#cite_note-KalasEtAl2020-22) A dinoflagellate cyst turnover in the eastern North Atlantic approximately ~2.60 Ma, during [MIS](/wiki/Marine_isotope_stages "Marine isotope stages") 104, has been cited as evidence that the NAC shifted significantly to the south at this time, causing an abrupt cooling of the North Sea and northwestern Europe by reducing heat transport to high latitude waters of the North Atlantic.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The [Isthmus of Panama](/wiki/Isthmus_of_Panama "Isthmus of Panama") developed at a convergent plate margin about 2.6 million years ago and further separated oceanic circulation, closing [the last strait](/wiki/Central_American_Seaway "Central American Seaway"), outside the polar regions, that had connected the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") and Atlantic Oceans.[[24]](#cite_note-24) This increased poleward salt and heat transport, strengthening the North Atlantic [thermohaline circulation](/wiki/Thermohaline_circulation "Thermohaline circulation"), which supplied enough moisture to Arctic latitudes to initiate the Northern Hemisphere glaciation.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) The change in the biogeography of the nannofossil *Coccolithus pelagicus* around 2.74 Ma is believed to reflect this onset of glaciation.[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, model simulations suggest reduced ice volume due to increased ablation at the edge of the ice sheet under warmer conditions.[[17]](#cite_note-Lunt082-17)

#### Collapse of permanent El Niño

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Collapse of permanent El Niño")]

A permanent [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño") state existed in the early-mid-[Pliocene](/wiki/Pliocene "Pliocene"). Warmer temperature in the eastern equatorial Pacific caused an increased water vapor [greenhouse effect](/wiki/Greenhouse_effect "Greenhouse effect") and reduced the area covered by highly reflective stratus clouds, thus decreasing the [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo") of the planet. Propagation of the El Niño effect through planetary waves may have warmed the polar region and delayed the onset of glaciation in the Northern Hemisphere. Therefore, the appearance of cold surface water in the east equatorial Pacific around 3 million years ago may have contributed to global cooling and modified the global climate's response to [Milankovitch cycles](/wiki/Milankovitch_cycles "Milankovitch cycles").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Rise of mountains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Rise of mountains")]

The elevation of continental surface, often as [mountain formation](/wiki/Mountain_formation "Mountain formation"), is thought to have contributed to cause the Quaternary glaciation. The gradual movement of the bulk of Earth's landmasses away from the [tropics](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics") in addition to increased mountain formation in the Late Cenozoic meant more land at high altitude and high latitude, favouring the formation of glaciers.[[29]](#cite_note-29) For example, the Greenland ice sheet formed in connection to the uplift of the west Greenland and east Greenland uplands in two phases, 10 and 5 Ma, respectively. These mountains constitute [passive continental margins](/wiki/Passive_margin "Passive margin").[[30]](#cite_note-Solgaardetal2013-30) Uplift of the Rocky Mountains and Greenland's west coast has been speculated to have cooled the climate due to jet stream deflection and increased snowfall due to higher surface elevation.[[17]](#cite_note-Lunt082-17) Computer models show that such uplift would have enabled glaciation through increased [orographic precipitation](/wiki/Orographic_precipitation "Orographic precipitation") and [cooling of surface temperatures](/wiki/Lapse_rate "Lapse rate").[[30]](#cite_note-Solgaardetal2013-30) For the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") it is known that the [Principal Cordillera](/wiki/Principal_Cordillera "Principal Cordillera") had risen to heights that allowed for the development of [valley glaciers](/wiki/Glacier_morphology "Glacier morphology") about 1 Ma.[[31]](#cite_note-Charrieretal1019-31)

## Effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Effects")]

The presence of so much ice upon the continents had a profound effect upon almost every aspect of Earth's hydrologic system. Most obvious are the spectacular mountain scenery and other continental landscapes fashioned both by glacial erosion and deposition instead of running water. Entirely new landscapes covering millions of square kilometers were formed in a relatively short period of geologic time. In addition, the vast bodies of glacial ice affected Earth well beyond the glacier margins. Directly or indirectly, the effects of glaciation were felt in every part of the world.

### Lakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Lakes")]

Further information: [Glacial lake](/wiki/Glacial_lake "Glacial lake")

The Quaternary glaciation produced more lakes than all other geologic processes combined. The reason is that a continental glacier completely disrupts the preglacial [drainage system](/wiki/Drainage_system_(geomorphology) "Drainage system (geomorphology)"). The surface over which the glacier moved was scoured and eroded by the ice, leaving many closed, undrained depressions in the bedrock. These depressions filled with water and became lakes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Glacial_lakes.jpg/250px-Glacial_lakes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glacial_lakes.jpg)

A diagram of the formation of the Great Lakes

Very large lakes were formed along the glacial margins. The ice on both North America and Europe was about 3,000 m (10,000 ft) thick near the centers of maximum accumulation, but it tapered toward the glacier margins. Ice weight caused crustal subsidence, which was greatest beneath the thickest accumulation of ice. As the ice melted, [rebound of the crust](/wiki/Post-glacial_rebound "Post-glacial rebound") lagged behind, producing a regional slope toward the ice. This slope formed basins that have lasted for thousands of years. These basins became lakes or were invaded by the ocean. The [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea")[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) and the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") of North America[[34]](#cite_note-34) were formed primarily in this way.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Quaternary_glaciation#Dubious "Talk:Quaternary glaciation")*]

The numerous lakes of the [Canadian Shield](/wiki/Canadian_Shield "Canadian Shield"), Sweden, and [Finland](/wiki/Finnish_Lakeland "Finnish Lakeland") are thought to have originated at least partly from glaciers' selective erosion of [weathered](/wiki/Weathering "Weathering") [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock").[[35]](#cite_note-Karnaetal1999-35)[[36]](#cite_note-UppslagsFiBERG-36)

#### Pluvial lakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Pluvial lakes")]

Main article: [Pluvial lake](/wiki/Pluvial_lake "Pluvial lake")

The climatic conditions that cause glaciation had an indirect effect on arid and semiarid regions far removed from the large ice sheets. The increased precipitation that fed the glaciers also increased the runoff of major rivers and intermittent streams, resulting in the growth and development of large pluvial lakes. Most pluvial lakes developed in relatively arid regions where there typically was insufficient rain to establish a drainage system leading to the sea. Instead, stream runoff flowed into closed basins and formed [playa lakes](/wiki/Playa_lake "Playa lake"). With increased rainfall, the playa lakes enlarged and overflowed. Pluvial lakes were most extensive during glacial periods. During interglacial stages, with less rain, the pluvial lakes shrank to form small salt flats.

### Isostatic adjustment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Isostatic adjustment")]

Main article: [Post-glacial rebound](/wiki/Post-glacial_rebound "Post-glacial rebound")

Major isostatic adjustments of the [lithosphere](/wiki/Lithosphere "Lithosphere") during the Quaternary glaciation were caused by the weight of the ice, which depressed the continents. In [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), a large area around [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay") was depressed below (modern) sea level, as was the area in Europe around the Baltic Sea. The land has been rebounding from these depressions since the ice melted. Some of these isostatic movements triggered large [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") in Scandinavia about 9,000 years ago. These earthquakes are unique in that they are not associated with plate tectonics.

Studies have shown that the uplift has taken place in two distinct stages. The initial uplift following [deglaciation](/wiki/Deglaciation "Deglaciation") was rapid (called "elastic"), and took place as the ice was being unloaded. After this "elastic" phase, uplift proceed by "slow viscous flow" so the rate [decreased exponentially](/wiki/Exponential_decay "Exponential decay") after that. Today, typical uplift rates are of the order of 1 cm per year or less, except in areas of North America, especially Alaska, where the rate of uplift is 2.54 cm per year (1 inch or more).[[37]](#cite_note-37) In northern Europe, this is clearly shown by the [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") data obtained by the BIFROST GPS network.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Studies suggest that rebound will continue for at least another 10,000 years. The total uplift from the end of deglaciation depends on the local ice load and could be several hundred meters near the center of rebound.

### Winds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Winds")]

The presence of ice over so much of the continents greatly modified patterns of atmospheric circulation. Winds near the glacial margins were strong and persistent because of the abundance of dense, cold air coming off the glacier fields. These winds picked up and transported large quantities of loose, fine-grained sediment brought down by the glaciers. This dust accumulated as [loess](/wiki/Loess "Loess") (wind-blown silt), forming irregular blankets over much of the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") valley, central Europe, and northern China. The trade winds over northern Africa intensified with the onset of Quaternary glaciation, evidenced by the increase in dust accumulation on the northwest African margin.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Sand [dunes](/wiki/Dune "Dune") were much more widespread and active in many areas during the early Quaternary period. A good example is the [Sand Hills](/wiki/Sandhills_(Nebraska) "Sandhills (Nebraska)") region in [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") which covers an area of about 60,000 km2 (23,166 sq mi).[[40]](#cite_note-40) This region was a large, active dune field during the Pleistocene epoch but today is largely stabilized by grass cover.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### Ocean currents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ocean currents")]

Thick glaciers were heavy enough to reach the sea bottom in several important areas, which blocked the passage of ocean water and affected ocean currents. In addition to these direct effects, it also caused feedback effects, as ocean currents contribute to global heat transfer.

### Gold deposits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Gold deposits")]

Moraines and till deposited by Quaternary glaciers have contributed to the formation of valuable [placer deposits](/wiki/Placer_deposit "Placer deposit") of gold. This is the case of [southernmost Chile](/wiki/Magallanes_Region "Magallanes Region") where reworking of Quaternary moraines have concentrated gold offshore.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Records of prior glaciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Records of prior glaciation")]

Main article: [Timeline of glaciation](/wiki/Timeline_of_glaciation "Timeline of glaciation")

See also: [Ice age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age") and [paleoclimatology](/wiki/Paleoclimatology "Paleoclimatology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Phanerozoic_Climate_Change.png/330px-Phanerozoic_Climate_Change.png)](/wiki/File:Phanerozoic_Climate_Change.png)

500 million years of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_(general_concept) "Climate change (general concept)").

Glaciation has been a rare event in Earth's history,[[44]](#cite_note-44) but there is evidence of widespread glaciation during the late [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic") Era (300 to 200 Ma) and the late [Precambrian](/wiki/Precambrian "Precambrian") (i.e., the [Neoproterozoic](/wiki/Neoproterozoic "Neoproterozoic") Era, 800 to 600 Ma).[[45]](#cite_note-45) Before the current ice age, which began 2 to 3 Ma, Earth's climate was typically mild and uniform for long periods of time. This climatic history is implied by the types of [fossil](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil") plants and animals and by the characteristics of sediments preserved in the [stratigraphic](/wiki/Stratum "Stratum") record.[[46]](#cite_note-46) There are, however, widespread glacial deposits, recording several major periods of ancient glaciation in various parts of the geologic record. Such evidence suggests major periods of glaciation prior to the current Quaternary glaciation.

One of the best documented records of pre-Quaternary glaciation, called the Karoo Ice Age, is found in the late Paleozoic rocks in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), South America, Antarctica, and [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). Exposures of ancient glacial deposits are numerous in these areas. Deposits of even older glacial sediment exist on every continent except South America. These indicate that two other periods of widespread glaciation occurred during the late Precambrian, producing the [Snowball Earth](/wiki/Snowball_Earth "Snowball Earth") during the [Cryogenian](/wiki/Cryogenian "Cryogenian") period.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## Next glacial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quaternary_glaciation&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Next glacial period")]

Further information: [Milankovitch cycles](/wiki/Milankovitch_cycles "Milankovitch cycles"), [Past sea level](/wiki/Past_sea_level "Past sea level"), [Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), and [Human impact on the environment](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Carbon_History_and_Flux_Rev.png/250px-Carbon_History_and_Flux_Rev.png)](/wiki/File:Carbon_History_and_Flux_Rev.png)

Increase in atmospheric CO  
2 since the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution")

The warming trend following the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum"), since about 20,000 years ago, has resulted in a [sea level rise](/wiki/Early_Holocene_sea_level_rise "Early Holocene sea level rise") by about 121 metres (397 ft). This warming trend subsided about 6,000 years ago, and sea level has been comparatively stable since the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic"). The present interglacial period (the [Holocene climatic optimum](/wiki/Holocene_climatic_optimum "Holocene climatic optimum")) has been stable and warm compared to the preceding ones, which were interrupted by numerous cold spells lasting hundreds of years. This stability might have allowed the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution") and by extension human [civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Based on [orbital models](/wiki/Milankovitch_cycles "Milankovitch cycles"), the cooling trend initiated about 6,000 years ago will continue for another 23,000 years.[[49]](#cite_note-Imbriel1980-49) Slight changes in the Earth's orbital parameters may, however, indicate that, even without any human contribution, there will not be another glacial period for the next 50,000 years.[[50]](#cite_note-Berger2002-50)
It is possible that the current cooling trend might be interrupted by an [interstadial](/wiki/Stadial "Stadial") phase (a warmer period) in about 60,000 years, with the next glacial maximum reached only in about 100,000 years.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Based on past estimates for interglacial durations of about 10,000 years, in the 1970s there was some concern that [the next glacial period would be imminent](/wiki/Global_cooling "Global cooling"). However, slight changes in the eccentricity of Earth's orbit around the Sun suggest a lengthy interglacial period lasting about another 50,000 years.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Other models, based on periodic variations in solar output, give a different projection of the start of the next glacial period at around 10,000 years from now.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Additionally, [human impact](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment") is now seen as possibly extending what would already be an unusually long warm period. Projection of the timeline for the next glacial maximum depend crucially on [the amount of CO  
2 in the atmosphere](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide_in_Earth%27s_atmosphere "Carbon dioxide in Earth's atmosphere"). Models assuming increased CO  
2 levels at 750 parts per million ([ppm](/wiki/Parts_per_million "Parts per million"); current levels are at 417 ppm[[54]](#cite_note-54)) have estimated the persistence of the current interglacial period for another 50,000 years.[[55]](#cite_note-Dynamic_Earth-55) However, more recent studies concluded that the amount of heat trapping gases emitted into Earth's oceans and atmosphere will prevent the next glacial (ice age), which otherwise would begin in around 50,000 years, and likely more glacial cycles.[[56]](#cite_note-LiveScience2007-56)[[57]](#cite_note-PIK2016-57)

Climate change may weaken the [Atlantic meridional overturning circulation](/wiki/Atlantic_meridional_overturning_circulation "Atlantic meridional overturning circulation") through increases in [ocean heat content](/wiki/Ocean_heat_content "Ocean heat content") and elevated flows of freshwater from melting [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet").[[58]](#cite_note-58) The [collapse of the AMOC](/wiki/Abrupt_climate_change "Abrupt climate change") would be a severe climate catastrophe, resulting in a cooling of the Northern Hemisphere.[[59]](#cite_note-59) It would have devastating and irreversible impacts especially for Nordic countries, but also for other parts of the world.[[60]](#cite_note-60)
